title: Tax-Efficient Investing: Complete Strategy Guide
description: Discover proven tax-efficient investing strategies, asset location rules, and ways to minimize investment taxes in your portfolio.

mainEntity.name: What is tax-loss harvesting and how does it work?

acceptedAnswer.text: Tax-loss harvesting involves selling securities at a loss to offset capital gains tax liability. If your losses exceed your gains, you can use up to $3,000 of those losses to offset ordinary income per year. It requires navigating the wash-sale rule, which prevents claiming the loss if you buy a substantially identical asset within 30 days.

Asset location is the strategy of placing different types of investments into specific accounts (taxable, tax-deferred, or tax-free) based on their tax efficiency. Highly taxed assets like REITs and bonds belong in tax-advantaged accounts, while tax-efficient index funds go in taxable accounts.

mainEntity.name: What are the wash sale rules?

acceptedAnswer.text: The IRS wash-sale rule prohibits you from claiming a loss on the sale of an investment if you purchase a 'substantially identical' stock or security within 30 days before or after the sale. If triggered, the loss is disallowed and added to the cost basis of the new purchase.

Qualified dividends are taxed at long-term capital gains rates (0%, 15%, or 20% depending on income), which are lower than ordinary income tax rates. To qualify, the dividend must be paid by a U.S. corporation or qualifying foreign entity, and you must hold the stock for more than 60 days during the 121-day period surrounding the ex-dividend date.

mainEntity.name: Long-term vs short-term capital gains tax rates?

acceptedAnswer.text: Short-term capital gains (assets held one year or less) are taxed as ordinary income, up to 37%. Long-term capital gains (assets held more than one year) benefit from lower tax rates of 0%, 15%, or 20%, depending on your taxable income and filing status.
